types.name: Character
types.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

types.name: Chekhov's Gun
types.description: An element introduced early that must become significant later — if shown, it must fire.

types.name: Event
types.description: A discrete occurrence that changes the state of the world.

types.name: Knowledge
types.description: An information relationship — what one entity knows about another.

types.name: Location
types.description: A physical space where events unfold and characters exist.

types.name: Motif
types.description: A recurring symbolic element — image, object, or pattern — that reinforces thematic meaning.

types.name: Novel
types.description: Long-form prose fiction structured in chapters and scenes.

types.name: Object
types.description: A physical thing with symbolic or narrative significance.

types.name: Occurrence
types.description: Where an event takes place (occurs-at, set-in).

types.name: Ownership
types.description: A possession relationship between an owner and a thing.

types.name: Participation
types.description: A character's involvement in an event (participates-in, witnesses, causes, suffers).

types.name: Interpersonal Relationship
types.description: An emotional or interpersonal bond between characters or between a character and an object.

types.name: Setup & Payoff
types.description: Something planted early that pays off later — the reader recognizes the callback.

types.name: Simple Lens
types.description: A single, non-composite narrative perspective.

types.name: Social Relationship
types.description: A professional or institutional relationship (employed-by, supervised-by).

types.name: Spatial Relationship
types.description: A spatial or containment relationship (located-in, part-of, confined-to, embedded-in).

types.name: Theme
types.description: An abstract idea that recurs across the narrative (e.g. alienation, duty, identity).

valueDefs.name: Branches Into
valueDefs.description: A frame relation where the timeline splits into a diverging parallel branch.

valueDefs.name: Canon
valueDefs.description: The canonical version of the story.

valueDefs.name: Character Bound
valueDefs.description: The narrator's perspective is anchored to a specific character.

valueDefs.name: Character Knowledge
valueDefs.description: The narrator knows only what the anchor character knows.

valueDefs.name: Character State
valueDefs.description: Emotional coloring derives from the anchor character's current emotional state.

valueDefs.name: Cliffhanger
valueDefs.description: The beat ends on an unresolved tension, leaving the reader in suspense.

valueDefs.name: Climax
valueDefs.description: The decisive turning point where the central conflict reaches its peak.

valueDefs.name: Colloquial
valueDefs.description: Language uses casual, everyday vocabulary reflecting informal speech.

valueDefs.name: Complex Nested
valueDefs.description: Sentences tend to be long with embedded subordinate clauses.

valueDefs.name: Continuation
valueDefs.description: The story continues directly from the previous beat without interruption.

valueDefs.name: Crisis
valueDefs.description: The moment of highest tension before the climax.

valueDefs.name: Cut
valueDefs.description: An abrupt, unadorned transition to the next beat.

valueDefs.name: Error
valueDefs.description: A constraint violation that represents a hard error — the story is invalid.

valueDefs.name: Fade
valueDefs.description: A gradual transition that softens the move to the next beat.

valueDefs.name: First Person
valueDefs.description: The story is told using first-person pronouns (I, we).

valueDefs.name: First Threshold
valueDefs.description: The moment the protagonist commits to the main journey.

valueDefs.name: Formal
valueDefs.description: Language uses elevated, precise vocabulary suited to formal register.

valueDefs.name: Full
valueDefs.description: The information is revealed completely.

valueDefs.name: High
valueDefs.description: Metaphors and figurative language are used frequently.

valueDefs.name: Hook
valueDefs.description: An opening beat designed to capture the audience's attention immediately.

valueDefs.name: Inciting Incident
valueDefs.description: The event that disrupts the protagonist's ordinary world and sets the story in motion.

valueDefs.name: In Moment
valueDefs.description: The narrator speaks from within the events as they unfold.

valueDefs.name: Linear
valueDefs.description: A single, unbranching timeline where time flows in one direction.

valueDefs.name: Low
valueDefs.description: Metaphors and figurative language are used sparingly.

valueDefs.name: Negative
valueDefs.description: Distortion diminishes or vilifies the target.

valueDefs.name: None
valueDefs.description: No metaphors or figurative language are used.

valueDefs.name: Objective
valueDefs.description: The narrator observes events externally without access to inner thoughts.

valueDefs.name: Omniscient
valueDefs.description: The narrator has access to all knowledge, including all characters' inner states.

valueDefs.name: Partial
valueDefs.description: Only part of the information is revealed.

valueDefs.name: Pinch Point
valueDefs.description: A beat where the antagonistic force asserts pressure on the protagonist.

valueDefs.name: Positive
valueDefs.description: Distortion amplifies or idealises the target.

valueDefs.name: Public Only
valueDefs.description: The narrator knows only publicly observable information.

valueDefs.name: Reliable
valueDefs.description: The narrator presents events accurately and without distortion.

valueDefs.name: Resolution
valueDefs.description: The beat where the conflict resolves and a new equilibrium is established.

valueDefs.name: Retrospective
valueDefs.description: The narrator looks back on past events from a later vantage point.

valueDefs.name: Rising Action
valueDefs.description: Beats that build tension and complexity as the story progresses.

valueDefs.name: Second Person
valueDefs.description: The story is told using second-person pronouns (you).

valueDefs.name: Selective
valueDefs.description: The narrator omits or downplays certain events or details.

valueDefs.name: Short Staccato
valueDefs.description: Sentences tend to be short and clipped, creating a staccato rhythm.

valueDefs.name: Third Person
valueDefs.description: The story is told using third-person pronouns (he, she, they).

valueDefs.name: Time Skip
valueDefs.description: The transition jumps forward (or backward) in time.

valueDefs.name: Unreliable
valueDefs.description: The narrator's account is distorted or intentionally misleading.

valueDefs.name: Warning
valueDefs.description: A constraint violation that is noteworthy but does not invalidate the story.

valueDefs.name: Act
valueDefs.description: A major division of a dramatic work.

valueDefs.name: Book
valueDefs.description: The top-level structural unit of a prose work.

valueDefs.name: Chapter
valueDefs.description: A named or numbered division of a narrative.

valueDefs.name: Film
valueDefs.description: The top-level structural unit of a screenplay or film.

valueDefs.name: Part
valueDefs.description: A major division within a book, grouping several chapters.

valueDefs.name: Scene
valueDefs.description: The smallest discrete narrative unit, occurring in a single location and time.

valueDefs.name: Sequence
valueDefs.description: A group of scenes forming a unified narrative unit.

valueDefs.name: Supervisor
valueDefs.description: A lens layer that oversees and moderates the output of other layers.

timeSystemType.name: Linear
timeSystemType.description: A single, unbranching timeline where time flows in one direction.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodes.name: Gregor Samsa
nodes.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

hasTag.name: Sympathetic
hasTag.description: A character who evokes the reader's compassion.

hasTag.name: Confinement
hasTag.description: Restriction of movement or freedom.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodes.name: Grete Samsa
nodes.description: Gregor's seventeen-year-old sister and the only family member willing to enter his room after the transformation. She feeds him, cleans his space, and learns his new preferences. Over time, the burden of care and the family's financial pressure erode her compassion. By the story's end she has stopped using Gregor's name, referring to him only as "it," and delivers the ultimatum that he must go.

hasTag.name: Family
hasTag.description: Related to the family unit and domestic bonds.

hasTag.name: Caretaker
hasTag.description: A character who assumes responsibility for another's wellbeing.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodes.name: Mr. Samsa
nodes.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

hasTag.name: Family
hasTag.description: Related to the family unit and domestic bonds.

hasTag.name: Authority
hasTag.description: Represents institutional or patriarchal power.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodes.name: Mrs. Samsa
nodes.description: Gregor's asthmatic mother who is torn between maternal love and visceral horror at his new form. She wants to help but faints at the sight of him, and her husband and daughter increasingly shield her from contact with Gregor. She represents the family's passive grief — neither actively cruel nor able to intervene on Gregor's behalf.

hasTag.name: Family
hasTag.description: Related to the family unit and domestic bonds.

hasTag.name: Sympathetic
hasTag.description: A character who evokes the reader's compassion.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodes.name: The Chief Clerk
nodes.description: Gregor's office supervisor who arrives at the apartment when Gregor fails to catch his morning train. His visit escalates the crisis: he threatens Gregor's job through the locked door, and when Gregor finally emerges in his insect form, the clerk flees without a word. He is the outside world's sole witness to the transformation, and his departure seals the family's isolation.

hasTag.name: Outsider
hasTag.description: Someone outside the family circle who intrudes on the domestic space.

hasTag.name: Authority
hasTag.description: Represents institutional or patriarchal power.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodes.name: The Charwoman
nodes.description: An elderly cleaning woman hired by the family after their previous cook leaves in disgust. Unlike the rest of the household, she is neither horrified nor sentimental about Gregor — she addresses him casually as "old dung beetle" and is the only person who opens his door without fear. She discovers his body in the morning and announces his death to the family with matter-of-fact cheerfulness. She disposes of the remains.

hasTag.name: Outsider
hasTag.description: Someone outside the family circle who intrudes on the domestic space.

hasTag.name: Pragmatic
hasTag.description: A character defined by practical, unsentimental action.

hasTag.name: Caretaker
hasTag.description: A character who assumes responsibility for another's wellbeing.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodes.name: The Three Lodgers
nodes.description: Three bearded gentlemen who rent a room in the Samsa apartment to supplement the family's income after Gregor can no longer work. They are fastidious, demanding, and authoritative — they effectively take over the living room and dictate household standards. When they discover Gregor during Grete's violin recital, they threaten to leave without paying and declare the apartment unsanitary. Their presence marks the family's final humiliation and triggers Grete's ultimatum.

hasTag.description: A character defined by practical, unsentimental action.

nodeType.name: Location
nodeType.description: A physical space where events unfold and characters exist.

nodes.name: Gregor's Room
nodes.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

nodeType.name: Location
nodeType.description: A physical space where events unfold and characters exist.

nodes.name: The Samsa Apartment
nodes.description: The modest Samsa family flat, chosen originally because Gregor's salary could cover the rent. The living room, kitchen, and hallway form the family's domain from which Gregor is excluded. As the family takes in lodgers for extra income, even this shared space contracts — the apartment itself becomes a measure of the family's shrinking world.

hasTag.name: Domestic
hasTag.description: Related to the household and private life.

hasTag.name: Family Space
hasTag.description: A space belonging to and defined by the family.

nodeType.name: Location
nodeType.description: A physical space where events unfold and characters exist.

nodes.name: The Living Room
nodes.description: The central shared space of the Samsa apartment. Before the transformation it is the family's gathering place; afterward it becomes territory contested between family and lodgers. The lodgers eat here, Grete plays violin here, and it is here that Gregor's final emergence causes the crisis that ends his life. The room's function shifts from domestic comfort to boardinghouse dining room — a spatial measure of the family's loss of control over their own home.

hasTag.name: Domestic
hasTag.description: Related to the household and private life.

hasTag.name: Contested Space
hasTag.description: A space whose control or meaning is disputed.

hasTag.name: Family Space
hasTag.description: A space belonging to and defined by the family.

nodeType.name: Object
nodeType.description: A physical thing with symbolic or narrative significance.

nodes.name: The Framed Picture
nodes.description: A magazine clipping of a woman in furs that Gregor laboriously cut out and placed in a gilded frame. When Grete and Mother begin removing the furniture from his room, Gregor throws himself onto the picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. It functions as the last tangible link to his human identity and desires.

hasTag.name: Decoration
hasTag.description: An ornamental object.

hasTag.name: Symbol
hasTag.description: An element carrying meaning beyond its literal function.

hasTag.name: Humanity
hasTag.description: Related to the retention or loss of human identity.

hasTag.name: Identity
hasTag.description: Related to who a character is or becomes.

nodeType.name: Object
nodeType.description: A physical thing with symbolic or narrative significance.

nodes.name: Grete's Violin
nodes.description: Grete's violin, which she plays with increasing skill over the course of the story. Gregor had secretly planned to announce at Christmas that he would fund her conservatory studies — a plan destroyed by the transformation. In the final part, Grete plays for the lodgers; Gregor, drawn by the music, crawls out of his room. This moment — where music briefly reconnects him to his human longings — directly triggers the lodgers' discovery and Grete's ultimatum that ends Gregor's life.

hasTag.name: Instrument
hasTag.description: A musical instrument.

hasTag.name: Symbol
hasTag.description: An element carrying meaning beyond its literal function.

hasTag.name: Humanity
hasTag.description: Related to the retention or loss of human identity.

nodeType.name: Object
nodeType.description: A physical thing with symbolic or narrative significance.

nodes.name: The Embedded Apple
nodes.description: An apple thrown by Father that lodges in Gregor's back during the attack. It is never removed and rots in place over the following weeks, becoming a festering wound that accelerates Gregor's physical decline. The family eventually grows so accustomed to it that they forget it is there. It serves as a permanent, visible marker of the family's violence — a wound carried by the victim while the perpetrators move on.

hasTag.name: Weapon
hasTag.description: An object used to inflict harm.

hasTag.name: Symbol
hasTag.description: An element carrying meaning beyond its literal function.

hasTag.description: Physical aggression or harm.

hasTag.description: Physical or moral deterioration over time.

nodeType.name: Object
nodeType.description: A physical thing with symbolic or narrative significance.

nodes.name: Father's Bank Messenger Uniform
nodes.description: The uniform Father wears to his new job as a bank messenger. He refuses to take it off at home, sleeping in it despite the family's protests. The uniform transforms him from a defeated old man into a figure of rigid authority — it is both a symbol of the family's forced self-reliance and of Father's new identity as household enforcer. Its gold buttons grow increasingly tarnished, mirroring the family's slow decline.

nodeType.name: Event
nodeType.description: A discrete occurrence that changes the state of the world.

nodes.name: The Transformation
nodes.description: Gregor Samsa wakes from uneasy dreams to find himself transformed into a monstrous insect. The event is presented without explanation or buildup — it simply is. The transformation is irreversible and sets every subsequent conflict in motion: loss of income, family shame, confinement, and the slow erosion of Gregor's place in the household.

nodes.name: The Chief Clerk's Visit
nodes.description: The chief clerk arrives at the Samsa apartment to investigate why Gregor has not reported for work. The family pleads through the locked door while Gregor struggles to operate the lock with his mandibles. When he finally opens the door, the clerk retreats in horror and flees the building. Father forces Gregor back into his room with a walking stick. This is the moment the transformation becomes a collective family crisis rather than Gregor's private problem.

nodes.name: The Furniture Removal
nodes.description: Grete and Mother begin emptying Gregor's room to give him more crawling space. Gregor initially welcomes the freedom but panics when he realizes his last human possessions are being taken. He throws himself onto the framed picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. Mother sees him clinging to the wall and faints, which triggers Father's apple attack. The scene crystallizes the tension between Gregor's insect needs and his human attachments.

nodes.name: The Apple Attack
nodes.description: After Gregor escapes his room during the furniture removal and frightens his mother into fainting, Father bombards him with apples from the fruit bowl. One apple lodges in Gregor's back and is never removed — it rots in place over the following weeks, becoming a festering wound. The attack marks the turning point from uneasy coexistence to active physical decline, and the embedded apple serves as a permanent reminder of the family's violence.

nodes.name: The Violin Recital
nodes.description: In the final part, Grete plays violin for the lodgers in the living room. The lodgers quickly lose interest, but Gregor — drawn irresistibly by the music — crawls out of his room into the living space. He fantasizes about telling Grete he would have sent her to the conservatory, a plan the transformation destroyed. The lodgers notice him, recoil in disgust, and threaten to leave without paying. This scene triggers Grete's declaration that the creature must go, leading directly to Gregor's death.

location.name: The Samsa Apartment
location.description: The modest Samsa family flat, chosen originally because Gregor's salary could cover the rent. The living room, kitchen, and hallway form the family's domain from which Gregor is excluded. As the family takes in lodgers for extra income, even this shared space contracts — the apartment itself becomes a measure of the family's shrinking world.

hasTag.name: Domestic
hasTag.description: Related to the household and private life.

hasTag.name: Family Space
hasTag.description: A space belonging to and defined by the family.

nodeType.name: Event
nodeType.description: A discrete occurrence that changes the state of the world.

nodes.name: Grete's Ultimatum
nodes.description: After the lodgers discover Gregor during the violin recital, Grete turns to her parents and declares: "It has to go. That's the only way, Father." She refuses to use Gregor's name, referring to the creature only as "it," and argues that they have done everything humanly possible. This is the moment the last family member who cared for Gregor formally renounces him — the final severance of his place in the household.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: Grete Samsa
participants.description: Gregor's seventeen-year-old sister and the only family member willing to enter his room after the transformation. She feeds him, cleans his space, and learns his new preferences. Over time, the burden of care and the family's financial pressure erode her compassion. By the story's end she has stopped using Gregor's name, referring to him only as "it," and delivers the ultimatum that he must go.

hasTag.description: Related to the family unit and domestic bonds.

hasTag.name: Caretaker
hasTag.description: A character who assumes responsibility for another's wellbeing.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: Mr. Samsa
participants.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

hasTag.name: Family
hasTag.description: Related to the family unit and domestic bonds.

hasTag.name: Authority
hasTag.description: Represents institutional or patriarchal power.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: Mrs. Samsa
participants.description: Gregor's asthmatic mother who is torn between maternal love and visceral horror at his new form. She wants to help but faints at the sight of him, and her husband and daughter increasingly shield her from contact with Gregor. She represents the family's passive grief — neither actively cruel nor able to intervene on Gregor's behalf.

nodeType.name: Location
nodeType.description: A physical space where events unfold and characters exist.

location.name: The Living Room
location.description: The central shared space of the Samsa apartment. Before the transformation it is the family's gathering place; afterward it becomes territory contested between family and lodgers. The lodgers eat here, Grete plays violin here, and it is here that Gregor's final emergence causes the crisis that ends his life. The room's function shifts from domestic comfort to boardinghouse dining room — a spatial measure of the family's loss of control over their own home.

hasTag.description: A space belonging to and defined by the family.

nodeType.name: Event
nodeType.description: A discrete occurrence that changes the state of the world.

nodes.name: Gregor's Death
nodes.description: Gregor dies alone in his room in the early morning hours, his body dried out and flat. The night before, Grete declared to the family that the creature is no longer Gregor and must be removed. Gregor, having overheard, retreats to his room and thinks of his family with tenderness before quietly expiring. The charwoman discovers his body and disposes of it. The family, relieved, takes the day off and rides the tram into the countryside, planning a smaller apartment and a fresh start.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: Gregor Samsa
participants.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

nodeType.name: Location
nodeType.description: A physical space where events unfold and characters exist.

location.name: Gregor's Room
location.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

source.name: The Framed Picture
source.description: A magazine clipping of a woman in furs that Gregor laboriously cut out and placed in a gilded frame. When Grete and Mother begin removing the furniture from his room, Gregor throws himself onto the picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. It functions as the last tangible link to his human identity and desires.

source.name: The Embedded Apple
source.description: An apple thrown by Father that lodges in Gregor's back during the attack. It is never removed and rots in place over the following weeks, becoming a festering wound that accelerates Gregor's physical decline. The family eventually grows so accustomed to it that they forget it is there. It serves as a permanent, visible marker of the family's violence — a wound carried by the victim while the perpetrators move on.

source.name: The Three Lodgers
source.description: Three bearded gentlemen who rent a room in the Samsa apartment to supplement the family's income after Gregor can no longer work. They are fastidious, demanding, and authoritative — they effectively take over the living room and dictate household standards. When they discover Gregor during Grete's violin recital, they threaten to leave without paying and declare the apartment unsanitary. Their presence marks the family's final humiliation and triggers Grete's ultimatum.

target.name: Grete's Violin
target.description: Grete's violin, which she plays with increasing skill over the course of the story. Gregor had secretly planned to announce at Christmas that he would fund her conservatory studies — a plan destroyed by the transformation. In the final part, Grete plays for the lodgers; Gregor, drawn by the music, crawls out of his room. This moment — where music briefly reconnects him to his human longings — directly triggers the lodgers' discovery and Grete's ultimatum that ends Gregor's life.

target.name: The Chief Clerk
target.description: Gregor's office supervisor who arrives at the apartment when Gregor fails to catch his morning train. His visit escalates the crisis: he threatens Gregor's job through the locked door, and when Gregor finally emerges in his insect form, the clerk flees without a word. He is the outside world's sole witness to the transformation, and his departure seals the family's isolation.

The room's function shifts from domestic comfort to boardinghouse dining room — a spatial measure of the family's loss of control over their own home.

source.name: The Charwoman
source.description: An elderly cleaning woman hired by the family after their previous cook leaves in disgust. Unlike the rest of the household, she is neither horrified nor sentimental about Gregor — she addresses him casually as "old dung beetle" and is the only person who opens his door without fear. She discovers his body in the morning and announces his death to the family with matter-of-fact cheerfulness. She disposes of the remains.

target.name: Gregor Samsa
target.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

hasTag.description: Restriction of movement or freedom.

source.name: Mr. Samsa
source.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

hasTag.description: Represents institutional or patriarchal power.

nodeType.name: Object
nodeType.description: A physical thing with symbolic or narrative significance.

target.name: Father's Bank Messenger Uniform
target.description: The uniform Father wears to his new job as a bank messenger. He refuses to take it off at home, sleeping in it despite the family's protests. The uniform transforms him from a defeated old man into a figure of rigid authority — it is both a symbol of the family's forced self-reliance and of Father's new identity as household enforcer. Its gold buttons grow increasingly tarnished, mirroring the family's slow decline.

nodeType.name: Event

target.name: The Transformation
target.description: Gregor Samsa wakes from uneasy dreams to find himself transformed into a monstrous insect. The event is presented without explanation or buildup — it simply is. The transformation is irreversible and sets every subsequent conflict in motion: loss of income, family shame, confinement, and the slow erosion of Gregor's place in the household.

location.name: Gregor's Room
location.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

nodeType.name: Event

target.name: The Chief Clerk's Visit
target.description: The chief clerk arrives at the Samsa apartment to investigate why Gregor has not reported for work. The family pleads through the locked door while Gregor struggles to operate the lock with his mandibles. When he finally opens the door, the clerk retreats in horror and flees the building. Father forces Gregor back into his room with a walking stick. This is the moment the transformation becomes a collective family crisis rather than Gregor's private problem.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: The Chief Clerk
participants.description: Gregor's office supervisor who arrives at the apartment when Gregor fails to catch his morning train. His visit escalates the crisis: he threatens Gregor's job through the locked door, and when Gregor finally emerges in his insect form, the clerk flees without a word. He is the outside world's sole witness to the transformation, and his departure seals the family's isolation.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: Mrs. Samsa
participants.description: Gregor's asthmatic mother who is torn between maternal love and visceral horror at his new form. She wants to help but faints at the sight of him, and her husband and daughter increasingly shield her from contact with Gregor. She represents the family's passive grief — neither actively cruel nor able to intervene on Gregor's behalf.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: Grete Samsa
participants.description: Gregor's seventeen-year-old sister and the only family member willing to enter his room after the transformation. She feeds him, cleans his space, and learns his new preferences. Over time, the burden of care and the family's financial pressure erode her compassion. By the story's end she has stopped using Gregor's name, referring to him only as "it," and delivers the ultimatum that he must go.

nodeType.name: Location
nodeType.description: A physical space where events unfold and characters exist.

location.name: The Samsa Apartment
location.description: The modest Samsa family flat, chosen originally because Gregor's salary could cover the rent. The living room, kitchen, and hallway form the family's domain from which Gregor is excluded. As the family takes in lodgers for extra income, even this shared space contracts — the apartment itself becomes a measure of the family's shrinking world.

target.name: The Chief Clerk's Visit
target.description: The chief clerk arrives at the Samsa apartment to investigate why Gregor has not reported for work. The family pleads through the locked door while Gregor struggles to operate the lock with his mandibles. When he finally opens the door, the clerk retreats in horror and flees the building. Father forces Gregor back into his room with a walking stick. This is the moment the transformation becomes a collective family crisis rather than Gregor's private problem.

nodeType.name: Character

participants.name: Gregor Samsa
participants.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

participants.name: The Chief Clerk
participants.description: Gregor's office supervisor who arrives at the apartment when Gregor fails to catch his morning train. His visit escalates the crisis: he threatens Gregor's job through the locked door, and when Gregor finally emerges in his insect form, the clerk flees without a word. He is the outside world's sole witness to the transformation, and his departure seals the family's isolation.

participants.name: Mr. Samsa
participants.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

participants.name: Mrs. Samsa
participants.description: Gregor's asthmatic mother who is torn between maternal love and visceral horror at his new form. She wants to help but faints at the sight of him, and her husband and daughter increasingly shield her from contact with Gregor. She represents the family's passive grief — neither actively cruel nor able to intervene on Gregor's behalf.

target.name: The Furniture Removal
target.description: Grete and Mother begin emptying Gregor's room to give him more crawling space. Gregor initially welcomes the freedom but panics when he realizes his last human possessions are being taken. He throws himself onto the framed picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. Mother sees him clinging to the wall and faints, which triggers Father's apple attack. The scene crystallizes the tension between Gregor's insect needs and his human attachments.

target.name: The Apple Attack
target.description: After Gregor escapes his room during the furniture removal and frightens his mother into fainting, Father bombards him with apples from the fruit bowl. One apple lodges in Gregor's back and is never removed — it rots in place over the following weeks, becoming a festering wound. The attack marks the turning point from uneasy coexistence to active physical decline, and the embedded apple serves as a permanent reminder of the family's violence.

nodeType.name: Event
nodeType.description: A discrete occurrence that changes the state of the world.

target.name: The Violin Recital
target.description: In the final part, Grete plays violin for the lodgers in the living room. The lodgers quickly lose interest, but Gregor — drawn irresistibly by the music — crawls out of his room into the living space. He fantasizes about telling Grete he would have sent her to the conservatory, a plan the transformation destroyed. The lodgers notice him, recoil in disgust, and threaten to leave without paying. This scene triggers Grete's declaration that the creature must go, leading directly to Gregor's death.

participants.name: The Three Lodgers
participants.description: Three bearded gentlemen who rent a room in the Samsa apartment to supplement the family's income after Gregor can no longer work. They are fastidious, demanding, and authoritative — they effectively take over the living room and dictate household standards. When they discover Gregor during Grete's violin recital, they threaten to leave without paying and declare the apartment unsanitary. Their presence marks the family's final humiliation and triggers Grete's ultimatum.

As the family takes in lodgers for extra income, even this shared space contracts — the apartment itself becomes a measure of the family's shrinking world.

source.name: Grete Samsa
source.description: Gregor's seventeen-year-old sister and the only family member willing to enter his room after the transformation. She feeds him, cleans his space, and learns his new preferences. Over time, the burden of care and the family's financial pressure erode her compassion. By the story's end she has stopped using Gregor's name, referring to him only as "it," and delivers the ultimatum that he must go.

target.name: The Violin Recital
target.description: In the final part, Grete plays violin for the lodgers in the living room. The lodgers quickly lose interest, but Gregor — drawn irresistibly by the music — crawls out of his room into the living space. He fantasizes about telling Grete he would have sent her to the conservatory, a plan the transformation destroyed. The lodgers notice him, recoil in disgust, and threaten to leave without paying. This scene triggers Grete's declaration that the creature must go, leading directly to Gregor's death.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: Gregor Samsa
participants.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

participants.name: The Three Lodgers
participants.description: Three bearded gentlemen who rent a room in the Samsa apartment to supplement the family's income after Gregor can no longer work. They are fastidious, demanding, and authoritative — they effectively take over the living room and dictate household standards. When they discover Gregor during Grete's violin recital, they threaten to leave without paying and declare the apartment unsanitary. Their presence marks the family's final humiliation and triggers Grete's ultimatum.

target.name: Grete's Ultimatum
target.description: After the lodgers discover Gregor during the violin recital, Grete turns to her parents and declares: "It has to go. That's the only way, Father." She refuses to use Gregor's name, referring to the creature only as "it," and argues that they have done everything humanly possible. This is the moment the last family member who cared for Gregor formally renounces him — the final severance of his place in the household.

participants.name: Mr. Samsa
participants.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: Mrs. Samsa
participants.description: Gregor's asthmatic mother who is torn between maternal love and visceral horror at his new form. She wants to help but faints at the sight of him, and her husband and daughter increasingly shield her from contact with Gregor. She represents the family's passive grief — neither actively cruel nor able to intervene on Gregor's behalf.

location.name: The Living Room
location.description: The central shared space of the Samsa apartment. Before the transformation it is the family's gathering place; afterward it becomes territory contested between family and lodgers. The lodgers eat here, Grete plays violin here, and it is here that Gregor's final emergence causes the crisis that ends his life. The room's function shifts from domestic comfort to boardinghouse dining room — a spatial measure of the family's loss of control over their own home.

target.name: Gregor's Death
target.description: Gregor dies alone in his room in the early morning hours, his body dried out and flat. The night before, Grete declared to the family that the creature is no longer Gregor and must be removed. Gregor, having overheard, retreats to his room and thinks of his family with tenderness before quietly expiring. The charwoman discovers his body and disposes of it. The family, relieved, takes the day off and rides the tram into the countryside, planning a smaller apartment and a fresh start.

location.name: Gregor's Room
location.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

source.name: The Transformation
source.description: Gregor Samsa wakes from uneasy dreams to find himself transformed into a monstrous insect. The event is presented without explanation or buildup — it simply is. The transformation is irreversible and sets every subsequent conflict in motion: loss of income, family shame, confinement, and the slow erosion of Gregor's place in the household.

source.name: The Chief Clerk's Visit
source.description: The chief clerk arrives at the Samsa apartment to investigate why Gregor has not reported for work. The family pleads through the locked door while Gregor struggles to operate the lock with his mandibles. When he finally opens the door, the clerk retreats in horror and flees the building. Father forces Gregor back into his room with a walking stick. This is the moment the transformation becomes a collective family crisis rather than Gregor's private problem.

participants.name: The Chief Clerk
participants.description: Gregor's office supervisor who arrives at the apartment when Gregor fails to catch his morning train. His visit escalates the crisis: he threatens Gregor's job through the locked door, and when Gregor finally emerges in his insect form, the clerk flees without a word. He is the outside world's sole witness to the transformation, and his departure seals the family's isolation.

participants.name: Mr. Samsa
participants.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

participants.name: Mrs. Samsa
participants.description: Gregor's asthmatic mother who is torn between maternal love and visceral horror at his new form. She wants to help but faints at the sight of him, and her husband and daughter increasingly shield her from contact with Gregor. She represents the family's passive grief — neither actively cruel nor able to intervene on Gregor's behalf.

participants.name: Grete Samsa
participants.description: Gregor's seventeen-year-old sister and the only family member willing to enter his room after the transformation. She feeds him, cleans his space, and learns his new preferences. Over time, the burden of care and the family's financial pressure erode her compassion. By the story's end she has stopped using Gregor's name, referring to him only as "it," and delivers the ultimatum that he must go.

location.name: The Samsa Apartment
location.description: The modest Samsa family flat, chosen originally because Gregor's salary could cover the rent. The living room, kitchen, and hallway form the family's domain from which Gregor is excluded. As the family takes in lodgers for extra income, even this shared space contracts — the apartment itself becomes a measure of the family's shrinking world.

source.name: The Furniture Removal
source.description: Grete and Mother begin emptying Gregor's room to give him more crawling space. Gregor initially welcomes the freedom but panics when he realizes his last human possessions are being taken. He throws himself onto the framed picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. Mother sees him clinging to the wall and faints, which triggers Father's apple attack. The scene crystallizes the tension between Gregor's insect needs and his human attachments.

participants.name: Gregor Samsa
participants.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

location.name: Gregor's Room
location.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

source.name: The Apple Attack
source.description: After Gregor escapes his room during the furniture removal and frightens his mother into fainting, Father bombards him with apples from the fruit bowl. One apple lodges in Gregor's back and is never removed — it rots in place over the following weeks, becoming a festering wound. The attack marks the turning point from uneasy coexistence to active physical decline, and the embedded apple serves as a permanent reminder of the family's violence.

source.name: The Violin Recital
source.description: In the final part, Grete plays violin for the lodgers in the living room. The lodgers quickly lose interest, but Gregor — drawn irresistibly by the music — crawls out of his room into the living space. He fantasizes about telling Grete he would have sent her to the conservatory, a plan the transformation destroyed. The lodgers notice him, recoil in disgust, and threaten to leave without paying. This scene triggers Grete's declaration that the creature must go, leading directly to Gregor's death.

participants.name: The Three Lodgers
participants.description: Three bearded gentlemen who rent a room in the Samsa apartment to supplement the family's income after Gregor can no longer work. They are fastidious, demanding, and authoritative — they effectively take over the living room and dictate household standards. When they discover Gregor during Grete's violin recital, they threaten to leave without paying and declare the apartment unsanitary. Their presence marks the family's final humiliation and triggers Grete's ultimatum.

source.name: Grete's Ultimatum
source.description: After the lodgers discover Gregor during the violin recital, Grete turns to her parents and declares: "It has to go. That's the only way, Father." She refuses to use Gregor's name, referring to the creature only as "it," and argues that they have done everything humanly possible. This is the moment the last family member who cared for Gregor formally renounces him — the final severance of his place in the household.

location.name: The Living Room
location.description: The central shared space of the Samsa apartment. Before the transformation it is the family's gathering place; afterward it becomes territory contested between family and lodgers. The lodgers eat here, Grete plays violin here, and it is here that Gregor's final emergence causes the crisis that ends his life. The room's function shifts from domestic comfort to boardinghouse dining room — a spatial measure of the family's loss of control over their own home.

source.name: Gregor's Death
source.description: Gregor dies alone in his room in the early morning hours, his body dried out and flat. The night before, Grete declared to the family that the creature is no longer Gregor and must be removed. Gregor, having overheard, retreats to his room and thinks of his family with tenderness before quietly expiring. The charwoman discovers his body and disposes of it. The family, relieved, takes the day off and rides the tram into the countryside, planning a smaller apartment and a fresh start.

nodeIds.description: A magazine clipping of a woman in furs that Gregor laboriously cut out and placed in a gilded frame. When Grete and Mother begin removing the furniture from his room, Gregor throws himself onto the picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. It functions as the last tangible link to his human identity and desires.

nodeType.name: Event
nodeType.description: A discrete occurrence that changes the state of the world.

nodeIds.name: The Transformation
nodeIds.description: Gregor Samsa wakes from uneasy dreams to find himself transformed into a monstrous insect. The event is presented without explanation or buildup — it simply is. The transformation is irreversible and sets every subsequent conflict in motion: loss of income, family shame, confinement, and the slow erosion of Gregor's place in the household.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

participants.name: Gregor Samsa
participants.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

hasTag.name: Sympathetic
hasTag.description: A character who evokes the reader's compassion.

hasTag.name: Confinement
hasTag.description: Restriction of movement or freedom.

nodeType.name: Location
nodeType.description: A physical space where events unfold and characters exist.

location.name: Gregor's Room
location.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

target.name: The Chief Clerk
target.description: Gregor's office supervisor who arrives at the apartment when Gregor fails to catch his morning train. His visit escalates the crisis: he threatens Gregor's job through the locked door, and when Gregor finally emerges in his insect form, the clerk flees without a word. He is the outside world's sole witness to the transformation, and his departure seals the family's isolation.

beats.name: The Door Opens
beats.description: The chief clerk arrives; Gregor manages to open his door and is seen by his family and the clerk, who flees. Father forces Gregor back into his room.

parent.description: A traveling salesman wakes as an insect and watches his family's love erode into revulsion, ending in his quiet death and their liberation.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodeIds.name: Mr. Samsa
nodeIds.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

hasTag.name: Family
hasTag.description: Related to the family unit and domestic bonds.

hasTag.name: Authority
hasTag.description: Represents institutional or patriarchal power.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodeIds.name: Mrs. Samsa
nodeIds.description: Gregor's asthmatic mother who is torn between maternal love and visceral horror at his new form. She wants to help but faints at the sight of him, and her husband and daughter increasingly shield her from contact with Gregor. She represents the family's passive grief — neither actively cruel nor able to intervene on Gregor's behalf.

hasTag.name: Family
hasTag.description: Related to the family unit and domestic bonds.

hasTag.description: A character who evokes the reader's compassion.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

nodeIds.name: Grete Samsa
nodeIds.description: Gregor's seventeen-year-old sister and the only family member willing to enter his room after the transformation. She feeds him, cleans his space, and learns his new preferences. Over time, the burden of care and the family's financial pressure erode her compassion. By the story's end she has stopped using Gregor's name, referring to him only as "it," and delivers the ultimatum that he must go.

nodeType.name: Location

nodeIds.name: The Samsa Apartment
nodeIds.description: The modest Samsa family flat, chosen originally because Gregor's salary could cover the rent. The living room, kitchen, and hallway form the family's domain from which Gregor is excluded. As the family takes in lodgers for extra income, even this shared space contracts — the apartment itself becomes a measure of the family's shrinking world.

nodeType.name: Event
nodeType.description: A discrete occurrence that changes the state of the world.

nodeIds.name: The Furniture Removal
nodeIds.description: Grete and Mother begin emptying Gregor's room to give him more crawling space. Gregor initially welcomes the freedom but panics when he realizes his last human possessions are being taken. He throws himself onto the framed picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. Mother sees him clinging to the wall and faints, which triggers Father's apple attack. The scene crystallizes the tension between Gregor's insect needs and his human attachments.

target.name: Gregor Samsa
target.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

beats.name: The Apple
beats.description: Gregor escapes his room during the furniture removal, terrifying his mother. Father bombards him with apples; one lodges in his back, a wound that will never heal.

parent.name: Gregor Samsa's Transformation
parent.description: A traveling salesman wakes as an insect and watches his family's love erode into revulsion, ending in his quiet death and their liberation.

nodeIds.name: Mr. Samsa
nodeIds.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

nodeIds.name: Mrs. Samsa
nodeIds.description: Gregor's asthmatic mother who is torn between maternal love and visceral horror at his new form. She wants to help but faints at the sight of him, and her husband and daughter increasingly shield her from contact with Gregor. She represents the family's passive grief — neither actively cruel nor able to intervene on Gregor's behalf.

nodeIds.name: The Embedded Apple
nodeIds.description: An apple thrown by Father that lodges in Gregor's back during the attack. It is never removed and rots in place over the following weeks, becoming a festering wound that accelerates Gregor's physical decline. The family eventually grows so accustomed to it that they forget it is there. It serves as a permanent, visible marker of the family's violence — a wound carried by the victim while the perpetrators move on.

nodeIds.name: The Apple Attack
nodeIds.description: After Gregor escapes his room during the furniture removal and frightens his mother into fainting, Father bombards him with apples from the fruit bowl. One apple lodges in Gregor's back and is never removed — it rots in place over the following weeks, becoming a festering wound. The attack marks the turning point from uneasy coexistence to active physical decline, and the embedded apple serves as a permanent reminder of the family's violence.

beats.name: The Junk Room
beats.description: Weeks after the apple attack, Gregor's room becomes a dumping ground. The family stops cleaning, the charwoman takes over his care, and the lodgers move in. Gregor eats less and less, his wound festers, and dust covers everything.

nodeIds.name: The Charwoman
nodeIds.description: An elderly cleaning woman hired by the family after their previous cook leaves in disgust. Unlike the rest of the household, she is neither horrified nor sentimental about Gregor — she addresses him casually as "old dung beetle" and is the only person who opens his door without fear. She discovers his body in the morning and announces his death to the family with matter-of-fact cheerfulness. She disposes of the remains.

nodeIds.name: The Three Lodgers
nodeIds.description: Three bearded gentlemen who rent a room in the Samsa apartment to supplement the family's income after Gregor can no longer work. They are fastidious, demanding, and authoritative — they effectively take over the living room and dictate household standards. When they discover Gregor during Grete's violin recital, they threaten to leave without paying and declare the apartment unsanitary. Their presence marks the family's final humiliation and triggers Grete's ultimatum.

nodeIds.name: Father's Bank Messenger Uniform
nodeIds.description: The uniform Father wears to his new job as a bank messenger. He refuses to take it off at home, sleeping in it despite the family's protests. The uniform transforms him from a defeated old man into a figure of rigid authority — it is both a symbol of the family's forced self-reliance and of Father's new identity as household enforcer. Its gold buttons grow increasingly tarnished, mirroring the family's slow decline.

nodeIds.name: Gregor's Room
nodeIds.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

target.name: The Living Room
target.description: The central shared space of the Samsa apartment. Before the transformation it is the family's gathering place; afterward it becomes territory contested between family and lodgers. The lodgers eat here, Grete plays violin here, and it is here that Gregor's final emergence causes the crisis that ends his life. The room's function shifts from domestic comfort to boardinghouse dining room — a spatial measure of the family's loss of control over their own home.

beats.name: It Has to Go

nodeIds.name: Mr. Samsa
nodeIds.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

nodeIds.name: The Charwoman
nodeIds.description: An elderly cleaning woman hired by the family after their previous cook leaves in disgust. Unlike the rest of the household, she is neither horrified nor sentimental about Gregor — she addresses him casually as "old dung beetle" and is the only person who opens his door without fear. She discovers his body in the morning and announces his death to the family with matter-of-fact cheerfulness. She disposes of the remains.

nodeIds.name: Grete's Violin
nodeIds.description: Grete's violin, which she plays with increasing skill over the course of the story. Gregor had secretly planned to announce at Christmas that he would fund her conservatory studies — a plan destroyed by the transformation. In the final part, Grete plays for the lodgers; Gregor, drawn by the music, crawls out of his room. This moment — where music briefly reconnects him to his human longings — directly triggers the lodgers' discovery and Grete's ultimatum that ends Gregor's life.

nodeIds.name: The Living Room
nodeIds.description: The central shared space of the Samsa apartment. Before the transformation it is the family's gathering place; afterward it becomes territory contested between family and lodgers. The lodgers eat here, Grete plays violin here, and it is here that Gregor's final emergence causes the crisis that ends his life. The room's function shifts from domestic comfort to boardinghouse dining room — a spatial measure of the family's loss of control over their own home.

nodeType.name: Event

nodeIds.name: The Violin Recital
nodeIds.description: In the final part, Grete plays violin for the lodgers in the living room. The lodgers quickly lose interest, but Gregor — drawn irresistibly by the music — crawls out of his room into the living space. He fantasizes about telling Grete he would have sent her to the conservatory, a plan the transformation destroyed. The lodgers notice him, recoil in disgust, and threaten to leave without paying. This scene triggers Grete's declaration that the creature must go, leading directly to Gregor's death.

nodeType.name: Character

participants.name: Gregor Samsa
participants.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

location.name: The Samsa Apartment
location.description: The modest Samsa family flat, chosen originally because Gregor's salary could cover the rent. The living room, kitchen, and hallway form the family's domain from which Gregor is excluded. As the family takes in lodgers for extra income, even this shared space contracts — the apartment itself becomes a measure of the family's shrinking world.

nodeIds.name: Grete's Ultimatum
nodeIds.description: After the lodgers discover Gregor during the violin recital, Grete turns to her parents and declares: "It has to go. That's the only way, Father." She refuses to use Gregor's name, referring to the creature only as "it," and argues that they have done everything humanly possible. This is the moment the last family member who cared for Gregor formally renounces him — the final severance of his place in the household.

nodeIds.name: Gregor's Death
nodeIds.description: Gregor dies alone in his room in the early morning hours, his body dried out and flat. The night before, Grete declared to the family that the creature is no longer Gregor and must be removed. Gregor, having overheard, retreats to his room and thinks of his family with tenderness before quietly expiring. The charwoman discovers his body and disposes of it. The family, relieved, takes the day off and rides the tram into the countryside, planning a smaller apartment and a fresh start.

location.name: Gregor's Room
location.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

nodeIds.name: The Framed Picture

It functions as the last tangible link to his human identity and desires.

nodeIds.name: The Transformation
nodeIds.description: Gregor Samsa wakes from uneasy dreams to find himself transformed into a monstrous insect. The event is presented without explanation or buildup — it simply is. The transformation is irreversible and sets every subsequent conflict in motion: loss of income, family shame, confinement, and the slow erosion of Gregor's place in the household.

source.name: Gregor Samsa
source.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

hasTag.name: Sympathetic
hasTag.description: A character who evokes the reader's compassion.

hasTag.name: Confinement
hasTag.description: Restriction of movement or freedom.

nodeType.name: Character
nodeType.description: A sentient being — human or transformed — that acts, speaks, or is acted upon.

target.name: The Chief Clerk
target.description: Gregor's office supervisor who arrives at the apartment when Gregor fails to catch his morning train. His visit escalates the crisis: he threatens Gregor's job through the locked door, and when Gregor finally emerges in his insect form, the clerk flees without a word. He is the outside world's sole witness to the transformation, and his departure seals the family's isolation.

edgeIds.description: Gregor works as a traveling salesman under the chief clerk's supervision to pay off his parents' debts

source.name: The Framed Picture
source.description: A magazine clipping of a woman in furs that Gregor laboriously cut out and placed in a gilded frame. When Grete and Mother begin removing the furniture from his room, Gregor throws himself onto the picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. It functions as the last tangible link to his human identity and desires.

target.name: Gregor's Room
target.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

payoff.name: The New Routine
payoff.description: Grete assumes the role of caretaker. The family adjusts uneasily. When Grete and Mother begin removing furniture, Gregor clings to the picture frame — his last vestige of humanity.

parent.name: Gregor Samsa's Transformation
parent.description: A traveling salesman wakes as an insect and watches his family's love erode into revulsion, ending in his quiet death and their liberation.

nodeIds.name: Grete Samsa
nodeIds.description: Gregor's seventeen-year-old sister and the only family member willing to enter his room after the transformation. She feeds him, cleans his space, and learns his new preferences. Over time, the burden of care and the family's financial pressure erode her compassion. By the story's end she has stopped using Gregor's name, referring to him only as "it," and delivers the ultimatum that he must go.

nodeIds.name: Mrs. Samsa
nodeIds.description: Gregor's asthmatic mother who is torn between maternal love and visceral horror at his new form. She wants to help but faints at the sight of him, and her husband and daughter increasingly shield her from contact with Gregor. She represents the family's passive grief — neither actively cruel nor able to intervene on Gregor's behalf.

nodeType.name: Event
nodeType.description: A discrete occurrence that changes the state of the world.

nodeIds.name: The Furniture Removal
nodeIds.description: Grete and Mother begin emptying Gregor's room to give him more crawling space. Gregor initially welcomes the freedom but panics when he realizes his last human possessions are being taken. He throws himself onto the framed picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. Mother sees him clinging to the wall and faints, which triggers Father's apple attack. The scene crystallizes the tension between Gregor's insect needs and his human attachments.

devices.name: The Apple

deviceType.name: Chekhov's Gun
deviceType.description: An element introduced early that must become significant later — if shown, it must fire.

devices.description: Father's growing hostility culminates in the apple bombardment. The apple lodged in Gregor's back rots over months, a physical marker of the family's violence that accelerates his decline toward death.

setup.name: The Door Opens
setup.description: The chief clerk arrives; Gregor manages to open his door and is seen by his family and the clerk, who flees. Father forces Gregor back into his room.

nodeIds.name: Mr. Samsa
nodeIds.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

nodeIds.name: The Samsa Apartment
nodeIds.description: The modest Samsa family flat, chosen originally because Gregor's salary could cover the rent. The living room, kitchen, and hallway form the family's domain from which Gregor is excluded. As the family takes in lodgers for extra income, even this shared space contracts — the apartment itself becomes a measure of the family's shrinking world.

nodeIds.name: The Embedded Apple
nodeIds.description: An apple thrown by Father that lodges in Gregor's back during the attack. It is never removed and rots in place over the following weeks, becoming a festering wound that accelerates Gregor's physical decline. The family eventually grows so accustomed to it that they forget it is there. It serves as a permanent, visible marker of the family's violence — a wound carried by the victim while the perpetrators move on.

nodeIds.name: The Apple Attack
nodeIds.description: After Gregor escapes his room during the furniture removal and frightens his mother into fainting, Father bombards him with apples from the fruit bowl. One apple lodges in Gregor's back and is never removed — it rots in place over the following weeks, becoming a festering wound. The attack marks the turning point from uneasy coexistence to active physical decline, and the embedded apple serves as a permanent reminder of the family's violence.

participants.name: Gregor Samsa
participants.description: A dutiful traveling salesman who has sacrificed his own desires to pay off his parents' debts. One morning he wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. Unable to work or communicate, he is confined to his room and watches helplessly as his family's initial shock gives way to resentment. His mind remains human even as his body and habits become increasingly alien, and he dies quietly after accepting that he is no longer wanted.

participants.name: Mr. Samsa
participants.description: The Samsa patriarch who had sunk into lethargy after his business failure, relying entirely on Gregor's income. The transformation forces him back to work as a bank messenger, and with his new uniform comes a renewed sense of authority. He is the most overtly hostile family member, twice driving Gregor back into his room by force — the second time bombarding him with apples, one of which lodges in his back and never heals.

devices.name: Violin Setup and Payoff

deviceType.name: Setup & Payoff
deviceType.description: Something planted early that pays off later — the reader recognizes the callback.

devices.description: Gregor's secret plan to fund Grete's conservatory studies is mentioned early, establishing the violin as a symbol of his human aspirations for his sister. The payoff comes in the final part when Grete plays for the lodgers and Gregor, drawn by the music, crawls out — the last flicker of his humanity directly causing his exposure and death sentence.

nodeIds.name: Gregor's Room
nodeIds.description: Gregor's bedroom, originally furnished with a desk, wardrobe, and the picture he framed himself. After the transformation it becomes his permanent confinement. Grete and Mother remove the furniture to give him crawling space, but the emptying also strips away his human markers. By the final months the room is used as a dumping ground for household junk, mirroring Gregor's own decline into irrelevance.

nodeIds.name: The Framed Picture
nodeIds.description: A magazine clipping of a woman in furs that Gregor laboriously cut out and placed in a gilded frame. When Grete and Mother begin removing the furniture from his room, Gregor throws himself onto the picture to prevent its removal — the one object he fights to keep. It functions as the last tangible link to his human identity and desires.

nodeIds.name: The Transformation
nodeIds.description: Gregor Samsa wakes from uneasy dreams to find himself transformed into a monstrous insect. The event is presented without explanation or buildup — it simply is. The transformation is irreversible and sets every subsequent conflict in motion: loss of income, family shame, confinement, and the slow erosion of Gregor's place in the household.

target.name: The Chief Clerk
target.description: Gregor's office supervisor who arrives at the apartment when Gregor fails to catch his morning train. His visit escalates the crisis: he threatens Gregor's job through the locked door, and when Gregor finally emerges in his insect form, the clerk flees without a word. He is the outside world's sole witness to the transformation, and his departure seals the family's isolation.

nodeIds.name: Grete Samsa
nodeIds.description: Gregor's seventeen-year-old sister and the only family member willing to enter his room after the transformation. She feeds him, cleans his space, and learns his new preferences. Over time, the burden of care and the family's financial pressure erode her compassion. By the story's end she has stopped using Gregor's name, referring to him only as "it," and delivers the ultimatum that he must go.
